nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, clear, warm lowland streams, ponds, and swampy areas with abundant aquatic vegetation and submerged woody debris; predominantly in the Milne Bay Province and southeastern Papua New Guinea

decorPreferences: caves and hollow ornaments for spawning (PVC pipe sections, ceramic caves, coconut shells)
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, cryptocorynes, floating plants for diffused lighting)
driftwood and leaf litter for natural cover and tannin release

diet.notes: The Peacock Gudgeon feeds primarily on live and frozen foods in the aquarium. Daphnia, baby brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, micro-worms, and small bloodworms are all suitable staples. High-quality frozen versions of these foods are generally accepted, and with patience most individuals can be trained to take micro-pellets and small sinking pellets as well. Particle size matters — large dry foods are generally refused. In the wild the species preys on small invertebrates and zooplankton.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, harlequin rasboras)
dwarf freshwater shrimp (with caution — fry may be eaten)
avoidWith: boisterous fast-swimming fish that out-compete at feeding
other bottom-dwelling territorial fish competing for caves

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by elcynico (1024x682). Tack-sharp adult male with fanned fins showing the full diagnostic suite — iridescent blue-grey body, rows of red/orange spots on the unpaired fins, yellow belly, and the dark ocellus 'peacock eye' at the tail base against a clean natural green backdrop. Best display shot.

Photo by Cisamarc (2828x2121). High-resolution vibrant male on a black background; the blue body, red-striped fins, orange bars, and caudal ocellus all pop dramatically. Different composition from the hero pick for variety.

Photo by Andrew Bogott (2472x1554). Male guarding a clutch of eggs and newly-hatched wrigglers on a leaf — documents the species' signature paternal cave/egg-guarding behavior. Behavioral extra; head is cropped out of frame so kept non-recommended.

summary: The Peacock Gudgeon (Tateurndina ocellicauda) is a vividly coloured nano sleeper-goby from Papua New Guinea. Males display an iridescent blue-grey body with red-spotted fins and a bold eye-spot at the base of the tail. Cave-spawning with dedicated paternal egg-guarding makes it among the most rewarding nano fish to breed.
careNotes:
The Peacock Gudgeon is a small sleeper-goby (family Eleotridae) endemic to Papua New Guinea — it is not a true goby (family Gobiidae), though the trade name 'Peacock Goby' is still widely used in the hobby. Males are among the more vividly coloured freshwater nano fish available, displaying an iridescent blue-grey body, rows of red and orange spots on the unpaired fins, a yellow belly, and a striking dark ocellus — commonly called the 'peacock eye' — at the base of the tail fin. Females are smaller, lack the pronounced rounded forehead hump that develops in mature males, and show less intense colouration on the fins.

A species tank or planted community aquarium of at least 60 L is recommended. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 5–15), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.8–7.8), warm (22–28°C), and well-filtered but with low flow — the species originates from slow, clear rainforest streams. Tannin-stained, dimly-lit setups with driftwood, leaf litter, and heavy planting closely replicate their native biotope and tend to encourage bolder behaviour and more natural breeding activity.

Spawning caves are essential: PVC tubes (approximately 2–3 cm in diameter), coconut cave ornaments, or purpose-made cave décor are all suitable. Males actively compete for and defend cave sites; providing at least one cave per male, plus additional sites, helps diffuse this rivalry. Live or high-quality frozen foods — daphnia, baby brine shrimp, bloodworms, micro-worms — should form the dietary staple, as dry foods alone rarely keep fish in peak condition. Avoid overly large or boisterous tankmates; the Peacock Gudgeon tends to hover in mid-water and is easily out-competed at feeding by more assertive fish. Despite a generally peaceful temperament toward other species, male rivalry over caves can be intense, and a well-decorated tank with visual breaks reduces the risk of injury.
breedingNotes: Peacock Gudgeons are cave-spawners and among the more readily bred nano fish in the hobby. A conditioned pair will generally spawn without special intervention beyond providing suitable cave sites and a diet of live or frozen food. The male fertilises eggs deposited inside the cave and assumes sole responsibility for guarding and fanning the clutch. If the male shows aggression toward the female after spawning, she should be removed, or a species-only breeding tank used from the outset. A clutch typically contains 50–150 pale, adhesive eggs, which hatch in approximately 7–10 days at 25°C. The male continues to guard the fry for several days after hatching. First foods for the fry are infusoria, green water, or a commercial liquid fry food for the first week, transitioning to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms as the fry grow. Fry are small and require very gentle water movement during the early weeks. Multiple spawns per year are common with well-fed adults, and the condition of the breeding adults — particularly whether they are fed regularly on live foods — noticeably affects both spawn frequency and fry survival rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tateurndina ocellicauda
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 7.5 cm), temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.8–7.8, hardness 36–215 ppm, native range (Papua New Guinea, Milne Bay and surrounding lowland drainages), biotope (slow clear vegetated lowland streams), spawning behaviour (cave-spawning, paternal egg guarding), and feeding notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Peacock Gudgeon Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-oriented care guide confirming tank size (20+ gallons / ~75 L recommended; 10 gal minimum for a pair), diet (live and frozen foods preferred, can train to dry), spawning cave requirements, and temperament (peaceful community fish). Notes the 'Peacock Goby' trade name misnomer — Eleotridae, not Gobiidae.

additional.name: FishBase — Tateurndina ocellicauda
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Eleotridae, Gobiiformes), native range (Papua New Guinea), maximum recorded size (7.5 cm TL), temperature range (22–28°C), and diet classification (carnivore). Confirmed lifespan approximately 4–6 years in captivity.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Peacock Goby species profile
additional.notes: Species profile confirming spawning behaviour details (male guards eggs 7–10 days to hatching), dimorphism notes (male cranial hump, female yellow belly stripe), and community compatibility. Confirms minimum tank 60 cm / 60 L for a pair.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Tateurndina ocellicauda feature
additional.notes: Historical hobbyist article on peacock gudgeon natural history and aquarium husbandry. Confirms PNG endemism, Eleotridae family placement, cave-breeding behaviour with paternal care, and notes on sexual dimorphism (male hump, red fin spotting more intense in males). Recommended as one of few vibrantly-coloured nano fish alternatives to dwarf cichlids.

fish.breedingNotes: The Peacock Gudgeon is a cave-spawner in which the male takes sole responsibility for guarding and fanning a clutch of 50–150 adhesive eggs for approximately 7–10 days until hatching. Fry are small and require infusoria or a commercial liquid fry food for the first week, transitioning to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms as they grow. Multiple spawns per year are common when adults are fed regularly on live or frozen foods. After spawning, the guarding male may become aggressive toward the female; removing her or providing a heavily decorated tank with plenty of hiding space helps reduce the risk of injury.
